text: CHITTRA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bahar, district and 60 m. NW of Ramgur.

CHITUL, or CHITULL, a town of Hindostan, in the peninsula of Gujerat, 90 m. NNE of Diu Fort, and 15 N of Umreylee. CHITUR, TCHITORE, or CHITTORE, a town and fortress of Hindostan, in the prov. of Rajputana, state of Mewar, in N lat. 24° 53', 65 m. ENE of Oodipur, on a hill near an affluent of the Bunass. It is one of the most important strongholds of the Hindus, and was formerly the cap. of the principality of Oodipur. It is still "what would be called in England a tolerably large market-town, with a good many pagodas, and a meanly built but apparently busy bazaar." [Heber.] The fortress rises immediately above the town, on a high rock, scarped by art all round the summit to the height of from 80 to 100 ft., and is surmounted by a rude wall with semicircular bastions enclosing an extensive area. It has a considerable trade in grain.—In 1303 this town was taken by the Mahommedans, and retained by them until 1345. It sustained a long and sanguinary siege by Akbar. Towards the close of the 16th cent., and in 1680, it was taken and to a great extent destroyed by Aureng-Zeyb. In 1790 it fell into the hands of Malorate-Madajèe-Sindiah, and was surrendered by him on the condition of an annual tribute to his sovereign. CHITUR, CHAITUR, or CHITTORE, a small town of Hindostan, in the prov. of the Carnatic, 78 m. WNW of Madras, on an affluent of the Pomey. CHITWAY, CHAITWYE, CHITWA, Or SETAVA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malabar, situated on an island named Chitway, or by the natives Manapuram, 10 m. W of Trichur, 39 m. NNW of Cochin, and in N lat. 10° 31'. This island, which is 27 m. in length, and in some parts 5 m. in breadth, is separated from the continent, and divided into two sections by navigable inlets of the sea. It is flat, and possesses little fertility of soil. Its principal production is cocoa-nuts.
CHIUARA', a river of Brazilian Guayana, which falls into the Rio Negro, between the town of Santa Isabel and Maracabi. Its banks were formerly inhabited by the Manaos Indians.
CHIUDUNO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 10 m. ESE of Bergamo, district and 2 m. SSE of Trescore. Pop. 1,156.
CHIURO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, deleg. and 6 m. E of Sondrio, district and 2 m. SE of Ponte, near the r. bank of the Adda. Pop. 2,500.

CHIUSA, a town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 8 m. SE of Coni, on the l. bank of the Pesio. Pop. 7,000. It is well built, and possesses manufactories of silk and glass. In the vicinity are the ruins of the castle of Mirabella.-Also a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, deleg. and 12 m. NW of Verona. It lies in a defile of the same name, CHI which forms the passage of the Adige and of one of the great roads from Tyrol into Italy. In this defile are still to be seen the ruins of the ancient fort by which it was formerly defended, and which was destroyed in 1801.—Also a town of Sicily, 35 m. SSW of Palermo, district and 12 m. SSW of Corleone. Pop. 6,002.

CHIUSA (LA), a village of Piedmont, in the prov. and 14 m. ESE of Susa, and 20 m. WNW of Turin, on the r. bank of the Dora-Riparia, in the Val-di- Susa. On the rocky peak of Monte Pirchiriano, which commands the Val-di-Susa, and at an elevation of 2,625 ft., is the celebrated and formerly rich abbey of San-Michele-della-Chiusa, founded in the 10th cent., and suppressed in 1622. It is now only used as an asylum for travellers.
CHIUSA-DI-BRESSANONE, or KLAUSEN, a town of Austria, in Tyrol, in the gov. of Inspruck, circle of Bolzano, on the Adige, 8 m. SW of Brixen. Pop. 698. Silver is wrought in the environs.
CHIUSA-DI-PLETZ, a fort of Illyria, in the gov. and 53 m. N of Trieste, circle and 32 m. N of Goritz, in a defile on the r. bank of the Isonzo.
CHIUSANO, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondaria, in the prov. of the Principato Ultra, district and 8 m. ENE of Avellino. Pop. 2,454. It contains a college, and has an annual fair.
CHIUSDINO, a town of Tuscany, in the comp. and 22 m. SW of Sienna, on the l. bank of the Mersa. Pop. 889.

CHIUSI, a town of Tuscany, cap. of a vicariato, in the comp. and 30 m. S of Arezzo, and 11 m. SE of Montepulciano, near the confines of the Pontifical states, and to the SE of the lake of the same name. It is situated on a hill, on the water-shed which separates the basins of the Chiana and Callone-di- Chiusi, at an alt. of 990 ft. above the level of the sea, and 485 ft. above that of the bed of the Chiana. Pop. in 1745, 1,133; in 1833, 2,226. This town, the Clusium of the Romans, and the Camars of the Etruscans, is one of the most ancient in Italy, and retains in its walls, catacombs, and museum of antiquities, many remarkable mementoes of its former importance. It was one of the 12 metropolitan cities of Etruria, and the cap. of the territory of Porsenna. In the Middle ages it became nearly depopulated by the pestilential marshes which were formed by the overflowing waters of the Chiana. Since these have been drained, the locality has recovered some measure of salubrity. C. is now the seat of the united bishopric of Chiusi-e-Pienza.—Lake C. lies on the confines of Tuscany and the States-of-the-Church. It is formed by the Chiana, and is about 5 m. in length. Its greatest breadth does not exceed 1 m.
CHIUSI-DEL-CASENTINO, a parish and village of Tuscany, in the comp. of Arezzo, 10 m. E of Poppi, near the ridge of the Apennines. Pop. 361. - In the vicinity is the celebrated monastery of Alvernia.

CHIVA, a town of Spain, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 22 m. W of Valencia. Pop. 3,954. It contains a parish church, an hospital, and a custom-house. In the environs are the ruins of a castle supposed to have belonged to the era of the Romans. The partido comprises 9 pueblos.
CHIVA. See KHIVA.

CHIVASSO, or CHIVAS, a town of Piedmont, cap. of a mandamento, in the prov. and 15 m. NE of Turin, in a fertile plain, near the l. bank of the Po. It was formerly one of the strongest places in Piedmont; a bastioned wall and ditch now form its only defence. It contains a public square, a church, a communal college, and 5 convents; and has a considerable trade in corn, cattle, and woollen fabrics. In 1639 this town was taken by Prince Thomas of year it fell into the hands of the allies. In 1800 it was again taken by the French.
CHIVELSTONE, a parish of Devon, 51 m. SSE of Kingsbridge, near Start bay. Area 2,720 acres. Pop. 591.
CHIVES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Aulnay. Pop. 1,128.
CHIYA'NEH, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Azerdbijan, district of Solduz, near the S extremity of Lake Urumiyah.
CHIZE', a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Deux-Sèvres, cant. of Brioux, 15 m. SW of Melle, on the r. bank of the Boutonne. Pop. 819. It contains some mines of iron.
CHKLIANTSI, or TCHKLIANTSY, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 57 m. N of Minsk, district and 45 m. NNW of Borisov.

CHKLOV, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 24 m. NNW of Moghilev, near the r. bank of the Dnieper. Pop. 2,500. The houses are constructed of wood, but the streets are regular; and it contains a well-built market-place, several churches, a synagogue, and three convents. Fairs are held three times a-year. The trade, which consists chiefly of imports from Germany, is carried on principally by the Jewish portion of the pop.
CHKOUDONI, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Vilna, district and 36 m. NW of Telsch, between the Barlaou and Louba. It contains a Lutheran church.
CHLEBNIKOVA, or KHLEBNIKOVA, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 18 m. NNW of Moscow, on the l. bank of the Khasma.
CHLINITZA, a town of Austria, in the Bukovine, on the r. bank of the Pruth, 12 m. WNW of Tchernowitz.
CHLOK, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Livonia, district and 20 m. W of Riga, on the l. bank of the Aa (Treider), and near the S bank of the gulf of Riga.
CHLOMIN, a town of Bohemia, in the circle and 33 m. NW of Kaurzim, and 16 m. N of Prague, 3 m. from the l. bank of the Elbe. It contains a castle.

CHLUMETZ, a town of Bohemia, cap. of a seignory of the same name, in the circle and 7 m. S of Neu-Bidschow, on the Cydlina, at an alt. of 642 ft. above sea-level. Pop. in 1833, 2,804. In the vicinity is the fine seignorial castle of Karlskron built in 1721, with extensive agricultural establishments belonging to the counts of Kinsky .- Also a v. in the circle and 23 m. E of Budweis, near the E frontier of Bohemia. It contains a castle, and has a glass and paper-work.—Also a v. and castle in the circle and 9 m. S of Beraun. Pop. 2,000. It is the cap. of the principality of Lobkowitz.
CHLUSSELBURG. See SCHLUSSELBURG.

CHMIELNIK, a town of Poland, in the woiwodship of Krakow, obwodie and 15 m. NNW of Stobnica. Pop. 1,800. It has several important fairs; and is noted as the scene of the defeat of the Poles by the Tartars in 1240. The district comprises a pop. of 16,695; and contains several copper-works, distilleries of brandy, and tanneries.
CHOA. See SHOA.
CHOBANDO, or SEBANDO, a district and town of Tibet, in Wei or Anterior Tibet, prov. and 230 m. ENE of Lassa.
CHOBHAM, a parish of Surrey, 41 m. SSE of Bagshot, 3 m. from the Southampton railway, and 26 m. from London. Area 9,470 acres. Pop. 1,989. The village is nearly encircled by the river Mole.

CHOC, a small island near the W coast of the island of St. Lucia, in Du Choc bay. CHO 548 Savoy, in 1705 by the French, and the following | the district of La Basseterre, on the bay and at the mouth of the river of the same name.
CHOCAYA-LA-NUEVA, a town of Bolivia, in the prov. and 110 m. SSW of Potosi, on the l. bank of the Cotagaita.

CHOCHA, a river of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Tver, which takes its rise near the village of Boneva, in the district of Zoubtzov, runs ENE, and forms for 24 m. the line of separation from the gov. of Moscow. After a total course of about 75 m., it throws itself into the Volga at Nisovka, and 17 m. E of Kortcheva.
CHOCHA, or Chocho, a town of Western Africa, in Upper Guinea, kingdom of Yariba, 15 m. NNE of Assonda.
CHO-CHAN, or Show-Chang, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Shan-tung, division of Yen-chu-fu, on the lake of the same name, which is traversed by the Yun-leang-ho or Imperial canal.
CHOCHOLNA, a village of Hungary, in the circle of Trentschin, on the Waag, noted for its acidulous springs.

CHOCLO - COCHA, a lake of Peru, in the intendancy of Ayacucho, prov. and 15 m. SSW of Castrovirema, in the Andes. The extreme coldness of its waters renders it destitute of fish. Its outlet forms the Calcamayo,—the principal head-stream of the Pampas.

CHOCO, a province of New Grenada, in the dep. of Cauca, comprising the valley of the Atrato, which flows N into the bay of Darien or C., in the Caribbean sea, and that of the San Juan, which flows SSW into the Pacific; and bounded on the E by the W branch of the Andes. It extends 144 m. from S to N, and 112 m. from E to W. Its climate is at once hot and humid; and the greater part of the surface is covered with dense forests. It produces maize, plantains, and cocoa of excellent quality. Gold and platina are amongst its mineral productions. Its chief town is Novita, on the Tamina, a branch of the San Juan, and 3 days by that river from the Pacific. At the junction of the Tamina, the San Juan is 400 yards wide; but it becomes rapidly narrower and shallower above Novita. Humboldt states that the curé of a village near Novita caused his Indian parishioners to dig a canal through the quebrada or ravine of Raspadura, in this prov., and that by this means canoes had actually passed, since 1788, between the two oceans, by means of the Atrato and the San Juan. See ATRATO. If such a communication actually exists, it can only be practicable during the rainy season. Capt. Cochrane, having ascended the San Juan, crossed the rising ground which divides the headstream of that river from the Citara, a branch of the Atrato, and found the distance to be about 400 yards, and the height of the ground to be cut through about 70 ft. But, after digging a few feet, he says, "you come to solid rock, which would make the undertaking expensive; besides it would be necessary to deepen each stream for a league." The Rapadura unites with the Atrato a little above the town of Citara, which stands on the r. bank of the united stream in the midst of swamps; and from this place to the mouths of the Atrato nearly the whole tract consists of unappropriated and perhaps uncultivable land. Capt. Cochrane embarked in a champan on the Atrato at Citara on the 12th of March, and sailing down that river passed the mouth of the Naipi or Napippi on the night of the 15th. Humboldt was informed that by means of this branch of the Atrato, which rises not far from the bay of Cupica on the Pacific, a watercommunication might be effected between the two oceans; and it is understood that surveys are now making to ascertain the practicability of this route.

CHOC (Du), a town of the island of St. Lucia, in But Cochrane was informed that the Napippi was shallow, rapid, and rocky; and that the land-carriage to the port of Cupica from this river must be conducted across three distinct lines of hills.
CHOANG-TSIN-POU, a town of China, in the district of Kan, in N lat. 39° 50', 40 m. NE of Sotchou.

CHOCO (BAY OF), a gulf of the Caribbean sea, into which the great river Atrato or Darien disembogues, and forming the southernmost part of the great gulf of Darien. It is usually called CANDELARIA BAY: which see .- Also an indentation of the coast of New Granada, between the parallels of 2° 30' and 4° 10' N; or, in its widest sense, between Point Guascama and Point Chirambira.
CHOCONTA, a town of New Granada, 30 m. NNE of Bogota, on the road to Tunja. Pop. 500.
CHOCONUT, a township in Susquehanna co., in Pennsylvania, U. S., 189 m. NE of Harrisburg. Pop. 962.
CHOCOPE, a village of Peru, in the prov. and 30 m. N of Truxillo, in the valley of the Chicania. It was devastated by an earthquake in 1759.
CHOCQUES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Bethune. Pop. 1,315.
CHOCTAW, a county in the state of Mississippi, U. S., situated a little N of the centre of the state, on the head-branches of Big Black river. Area 920 sq. m. The cap. is Greensboro'. Pop. 6,010.

CHOCTAWS, or CHOCTAS, an extensive tribe of North American Indians, originally settled to the S of the Chickasaws, between the Mississippi and the Tombecbee, and speaking a dialect of the Mobilian so nearly resembling that of the Chickasaws that they almost seemed but one nation, though, unlike the latter, they were the allies of the French. They are now located to the E of the Chickasaws, in the same territory on the W of the Mississippi; bounded on the N by the Canadian and Arkansas rivers; on the E by the state of Arkansas; on the S by Red river; and on the W by the western territories of the United States,—a district of country about 200 m. long, and 150 m. broad. Their numbers are estimated at nearly 20,000. They are extensively engaged in agriculture, and have good houses and well-enclosed fields. They raise large quantities of Indian corn; and in the southern part of their settlements, considerable cotton. They have 9 cotton gins, and several grist and saw-mills, erected on the Red river and other streams; and they possess large stocks of cattle, horses, sheep, and swine. They are governed by a written constitution and laws. The nation is divided inte four districts, each of which elects a chief every 4 years. The general council consists of 40 members, chosen by the qualified voters of each district, and assembles on the first Monday of October annually. The council passes the laws; the chiefs have a veto power, which can be overruled by a vote of two-thirds of the council. The council chooses its speaker; clerks record the proceedings; and the speaker is addressed, and the business transacted, with the customary forms of legislative proceedings. The council generally continues its session about two weeks, and the members are paid, from the funds of the nation, 2 dollars a-day. They have a large and commodious council-house. The nation is divided into judicial districts; and trial by jury, and appeal to the highest judicial tribunal, are practised. There is no enforcement of the payment of debts; this is left to honour, which is generally observed. The military department of the nation is intrusted to a general elected by the people, with 32 captains in each district. Spinning and weaving are carried on in many parts of the country; blacksmiths are furnished by the United States, according to treaty stipulations; many of the principals, and all the assistants, belonging to the Indians. The C. may be regarded as among the most intelligent of the Indian tribes. It is their boast, say the American commissioners of Indian affairs, that in war they never shed the blood of an American. They have frequently entered the military service of the United States. They voluntarily appropriate 18,000 dollars of their annuity from the United States government to the support of schools amongst themselves; and they have recently founded an academical institution in connexion with the Baptist mission amongst them, and also a female seminary. - Congress Papers.

CHOCKTAWHATCHEE, a river which takes its rise in the state of Alabama, U. S.; runs SSW; enters Florida; and after a total course of 130 m., falls into Choctawhatchee or St. Rosa bay, in the gulf of Mexico.

CHOCZ, or CHOCZA, a town of Poland, in the gub., obwod, and 18 m. NNW of Kalisz, on the r. bank of the Prosna. Pop. 1,200. NAMED AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AN CHO CHOCZIM, KHOCIN, or KHOTIN, a town and fortress of European Russia, in the gov. of Bessarabia. situated on a hill on the r. bank of the Dniester, 15 m. SSW of Kaminiec. It stands near the frontier of ancient Poland, and was formerly included in the Turkish province of Moldavia. Pop. in 1833, 9,869. The town was at first defended only by a castle; but in 1718 it was converted, by French engineers, into a regular fortress of great strength. C., formerly celebrated for several victories gained over the Turks by the Poles, in 1739 became remarkable for a great battle between the Russians and the Turks, which led to the surrender of the place to the former on the 31st of August. Being restored at the peace, it became again an object of attack in the following war; and, after another great battle gained by Prince Gallitzin, fell into the hands of the Russians, on 20th Sept. 1769, and remained in their possession till the peace of Kainarji, in 1774. In 1788 the united Russian and Austrian armies, under the prince of Cobourg, invested C., and having reduced the garrison by famine, forced it to surrender on 19th Sept. The place reverted once more to the Turks at the peace of Jassy in 1792, but in 1812 was ceded to the Russians by treaty, along with Bessarabia, and a portion of Moldavia.
CHODACZOW, a town of Austria, in the prov. of Galicia, district and 4 m. WSW of Tarnopol.
CHODECZ, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Mazovie, obwod of Kouiavie, 15 m. SSE of Brzesc, on an affluent of the Zglowionezka. Pop. 400.
CHODEL, a town of Poland, in the gov., obwod, and 22 m. WSW of Lublin, on an affluent of the Vistula.
CHODERKOWCE, a town of Austria, in the prov. of Galicia, gov. and 22 m. SSE of Lemberg, on an affluent of the Vistula.
CHODOROW, a town of Galicia, in the gov. and 33 m. SSE of Lemberg, circle of Brzezany, on an affluent of the Vistula and 14 m. S of Choderkowce.

CHODZIESEN, a town of Prussia, cap. of a circle of the same name, in the gov. and 42 m. N of Posen, and 45 m. WSW of Bromberg, on a lake which discharges itself into the Netz. Pop. in 1837, 2,925, of whom 1,000 were Jews. It contains a Lutheran and three Catholic churches, a synagogue, and an hospital; and has manufactories of cloth, linen, lace, &c., and several tanneries and dye-works. Its trade consists chiefly in grain.
CHOGDAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, district of Nuddea, on the E bank of the Hooghly, 34 m. N of Calcutta.
CHOGHYE, a town of Afghanistan, in the district of Gurmseer, 165 m. SW of Kandahar.

CHOISEUL, an island of the South Pacific, in the Solomon group, between Bougainville island on the WNW and Ysabel on the ESE, from the latter of which it is separated by Pitt's strait. It extends about 60 m. from ESE to NNW, and has an average breadth of 9 m. Cape Cornwallis forms an extensive promontory on its N coast, and near its E extremity is a lofty summit named Gros Morne.

CHOISEUL (PORT), a port of Madagascar, on the E coast, on the NW side of Antongil bay, and at the mouth of the Lingebate. It was formerly a French establishment, founded by Beniowsky and named Louisbourg.

CHOISY-EN-BRIE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Marne, cant. of La-Ferté-Gaucher, 8 m. ESE of Coulommiers. Pop. 1,304. It was formerly fortified. In the vicinity is a tile-work.

CHOISY-LE-ROI, or CHOISY-SUR-SEINE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine, cant. of Villejuif, 8 m. S of Paris, beautifully situated on the l. bank of the Seine-which is here crossed by an old wooden bridge—and on the Orleans railway. Pop. in 1841, 3,119; in 1846, 3,277. Its church, built by Louis XV., is a handsome edifice, and the environs are adorned with numerous villas. It contains extensive manufactories of fine china, glass, morocco leather, chemical substances, soap, &c. It formerly contained a castle in which Louis XV. frequently resided.
CHOL (SANTA-CRUZ-DEL), an Indian settlement, in the prov. of Vera Paz, in Guatimala, between the Rio Grande and the Rabinal.
CHOKA. See TARAKAI.
CHOKEYGUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwa, circar of Raisseen, on a hill 50 m. WSW of Bhopal.

CHOKIER, or CHOCKIER, a town of Belgium, in the prov. and 8 m. SW of Liege, cant. of Hologne, on the l. bank of the Maas. Pop. 451. It contains a fine castle, and in the environs are extensive quarries of limestone.
CHOKOUR, a village of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. of Anatolia, 15 m. SSE of Denizli, in a profound valley full of pines.
CHOLA-VENDAM, a large town of Hindostan, in the collectorship of Madura, in N lat. 10° 1', E long. 78° 5', on the N bank of the Vayar.
CHOLDERTON (EAST), a tything in the p. of Amport, Hants. Pop. 328.
CHOLDERTON (WEST), a parish of Wilts, 5 m. ENE of Amesbury. Area 1,390 acres. Pop. 170.

CHOLEECHEL, or Isla-de-Rosas, a large island, or rather a group of islands, in the Rio Negro of the La Plata states, intersected by the parallel of 39° S, and the meridian of 66° 10' W long. It is here that the Aucazes Indians, in their journeys from the Cordillera, leave the course of the Negro, and strike NNE to the Colorado. A military station has been formed here for the protection of the S frontier of Buenos Ayres.
CHOL-HOTUN, a city of Mandshuria, in N lat. 43° 59', and long. E of Peking 1° 26' 40".
CHOLL, a village of Sinde, 42 m. NW of Sehwan, in N lat. 26° 55', on a branch of the Nara.
CHOLLERTON, a parish and township of Northumberland, 6 m. N of Hexham, bounded on the W by the Tyne. Area 16,020 acres. Pop. 1,129; of township, 393.

CHOLLET, or CHOLET, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, arrond. of Beaupreau. The cant. comprises 12 com. Pop. in 1831, 20,462; in 1841, 22,110. The town is pleasantly situated on the r. bank of the Moine, 15 m. SSE of Beaupreau. Pop. in 1831, 7,345; in 1846, 8,413. It contains a fine castle and a communal college, and has extensive manufactories of cotton, linen, and woollen fabrics, cotton and woollen spinning-mills, bleacheries, dye-works, and tanneries. The trade, which is extremely active, consists in horses, cattle, pigs, corn, lint, thread, and articles, particularly handkerchiefs, of local manufacture. Fairs are held five times a-year. The town was several times taken, and to a great extent destroyed, during the wars of La Vendee, in 1793 and 1795.
CHOLMONDELEY, a township in the p. of Malpas, Cheshire, 7 m. W of Nantwich. Area 2,480 acres. Pop. 260.
CHOLMONDSTONE, a township in the p. of Acton, Cheshire. Area 1,470 acres. Pop. 206.
CHOLMONDELEY'S SOUND, a passage on the E coast of the Prince of Wales archipelago, in Clarence strait, with a small island at its NW extremity, in N lat. 55° 15'.
CHOLOGOW, a town of Galicia, in the circle and 35 m. NNW of Zloczow, and 35 m. NNE of Lem- CHO berg. Pop. 1,550. It contains a castle and two Greek churches.
CHOLOY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Meurthe, cant. and 4 m. from Toul. Pop. 450. It contains extensive nursery gardens.
CHOLSEY, a parish of Berks, 2½ m. SW of Wallingford, intersected by the Great Western railway. Area 5,060 acres. Pop. 1,191.
CHOLTIZ, a town of Bohemia, in the circle and 9 m. NW of Chrudim. It contains a castle, and about 182 houses.

CHOLULA, a city of Mexico, 80 m. E of the city of Mexico, 9 m. W of Puebla; in N lat. 19° 2' 6", some miles to the l. or S of the main road to Mexico, in a broad plain which extends to the base of the chain in advance of the great Nevados, and at an alt. of 6,912 ft. above sea-level. It was formerly the cap. of an independent district of the same name, which, along with the republics of Tlaxcala, and Guejozingo or Huetxocingo, resisted the Mexican power for several centuries. It is now in the state of Puebla. Its principal square is spacious, and it contains several large churches; but it is now "scarcely more than a village, and seems gradually still more decaying." It is surrounded by neatly cultivated gardens, and fields of corn and maguay. C. was called, by Cortes, Churultecal; and contained, at the era of the Spanish conquest, 20,000 houses, exclusive of the adjoining villages or suburbs, which were computed to contain as many more. It is now inhabited exclusively by Indians, who number about 16,000 [Porter]; 6,000 [Poinsett]. Cortes, in his despatches to the court of Madrid, gives a curious description of this city.— "The inhabitants," he observes, "are better clothed than any we have hitherto seen. People in easy circumstances wear cloaks above their dress. The environs of the city are fertile and well-cultivated. Almost all the fields may be watered; and the city is much more beautiful than most of those in Spain, for it is well-fortified, and built on very level ground. The number of the inhabitants is so great that there is not an inch of ground uncultivated; and yet, in several places, the Indians experience the effects of famine, and there are many beggars who ask alms from the rich." Its commerce consisted in manufactures of cotton, gems, and plates of clay; and it was famed for its jewellers and potters. When the Mexican empire was in its glory, C. was revered as the seat of religion. Its numerous temples-of which Cortes counted more than 100—and more especially the great temple erected on an artificial mountain, which still remains, attracted innumerable pilgrims from the most distant provinces to perform their devotions on this consecrated spot; and since the introduction of Christianity among the Indians, the symbols of a new worship have not entirely effaced the remembrance of the old. The people still assemble in crowds from distant quarters, at the summit of one of its three teocallis or pyramids, to celebrate the festival of the Virgin, in a church in which only priests of Indian descent officiate. The appearance of this teocalli is that of a natural hill rising out of a plain, having a pyramidal form, and covered to its summit with shrubs and trees. The waste of centuries has rounded and broken down its angles, and confounded its terraces: when examined, however, it is found to consist of distinct and alternate layers of adobe or unburnt bricks, "interspersed with occasional layers of plaster and stone work." [Mayer.] The platform on the summit is protected by a parapet. We are informed by Humboldt, who examined it, that it has four stages all of equal height, and that it appears to have been constructed exactly in the direction of the four cardinal points; but it is so covered with vegetation that it is difficult to ascertain their primitive direction. The perpendicular i height of this edifice is 162 ft. [Humboldt], 177 ft. [Latrobe]; at the base it measures on each side 1,450 ft. [Humboldt], 1,425 ft. [Latrobe]. The platform on the top, which measures about 16,000 sq. ft. [Humboldt], 3,500 sq. yards [Poinsett], is reached by a rugged flight of stone steps. Taking its base as a square of 480 yards on the side, and assuming that its height was one-eighth of the base line, or 180 ft.; that the slopes were formed at an angle of 45°; and that this truncated pyramid was divided into its four stages by means of three terraces each 30 yards in breadth, Captain Vetch estimates its solid contents at 7,146,000 cubic yards. The two other teocalli in its vicinity are of comparatively small dimensions. It is supposed to have been used both as a temple and a tomb: for a road which has been cut through a portion of it, laid open a cavity containing two skeletons, a number of idols in basalt, and several vases. "The Indians tell you that it was a place of sepulture; and the Mexicans give you the universal reply of ignorance in this country, -Quien sabe,- 'who knows,'-'who can tell.'" [Mayer.] The view from the summit is grand and beautiful, embracing the town and its surrounding fertile plains, the volcanoes of Puebla, the mountains of Tlascala on the N, and the detached nevados or snowy peaks of Popocatepetl, Iztacihuatl, and Orizaba, each of which exceeds Mont Blanc in elevation. The subjoined sketch of this remarkable monument is from the W, and was taken by Mr. B. Mayer in 1841. Cortes, in his advance to Mexico with his 600 Spaniards, stopped at C., and was to all appearance kindly received; but having received intelligence that a plot was laid against the life of himself and followers, he took a severe revenge, by massacring a number of the principal citizens, and setting fire to the houses.
CHOMEANE, a mountain of France, in the dep. of the Drôme, cant. of Oust, 15 m. SE of Valence. It runs from N to S parallel to the mountains of Lozeron and Boussiere.
CHOMELIX, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Haute Loire, cant. of Craponne, 18 m. N of Le Puy, on the l. bank of the Arzon. Pop. 1,481.

CHOMERAC, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, arrond. of Privas. The cant. comprises 7 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,278; in 1841, 8,237. The town is on the r. bank of the Maldarie, 4 m. SE of Privas. Pop. in 1846, 2,505.
CHOMONCHOAN, a lake of Lower Canada, in the Saguenay district, to the N of Assuapmoussoin lake. It is about 30 m. in length, and 15 in breadth.
CHOMPNA, a village of Hindostan, in Malwah, 17 m. from Pertaubghur. Opium is extensively cultivated in the environs.
CHONAD. See CSANAD.

CHONES, a river of New Grenada, in the intendancy of Ecuador, which takes its rise in the prov. of Pichincha, runs W, receives the Congoma, and after a total course of 60 m. falls into Caragues bay, 15 m. SSE of Cape Pasado. On its N bank, 20 m. from its mouth, is a small town of the same name. CHO
CHONGOA, an Indian town of New Grenada, in the intendancy and 12 m. SW of Guayaquil, on a small stream which flows into the gulf of Guayaquil.

CHONOS ARCHIPELAGO, an extensive group of islands off the W coast of S America, intersected by the parallel of 45° S. They are chiefly composed of a stratified, soft littoral deposit. Their climate approaches that of Tierra-del-Fuego; and their prevailing vegetation consists of mosses, lichens, and small ferns. CHOO. It may be necessary again to remind the reader that the double o is generally written u in the columns of the present work.
CHOPER. See KHOPER.
CHOPERSK. See KHOPERSK.
CHOPO, a town of New Granada, in the prov. and 10 m. N by W of Pamplona.

CHOPO, a small town of S Africa, in the Bichuanas country, in about S lat. 27° 26', on the N side of a river flowing SW called the Nokannan. When the missionary, Campbell, visited this town in the month of July, the bed of the river was dry, but water was found in it on digging to a depth of 25 ft. The therm. at sunrise was 37°; at noon, 65°.

CHOPOTO, or XIPOTO, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes; rising in the Sierra Mantiqueira, 25 m. E of Barbacena; running N and NE for about 70 m.; receiving the Pirangas on the l., and the Turvo on the r.; and assuming the name of the Rio Doce below the salto or fall of Inferno .- There is a parish and v. of the same name on the upper part of the river.
CHOPRAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Candeish, in N lat. 21° 41′, 58 m. W of Burhanpur, within a few miles of the r. bank of the Tapti.
CHOPRAH. See CHUPRAH.

CHOPTANK, a river of Maryland, U. S., formed by the union of the Tuckaho, and an E branch rising in Delaware. It flows into Chesapeake bay, on the E side, in N lat. 38° 30', and is navigable 40 m. CHOPUNISH, SAPTIN, or NEZ-PE'RCES, a tribe of Indians in N America, who possess the country on each side of Lewis or Snake river, from the Peloose to the Wapticacoes, a distance of 100 m., and extend E to the foot of the Rocky mountains. They are supposed to number about 2,000, and are said to be superior to the surrounding tribes in intellect and in moral qualities. Their complexion is darker than that of some neighbouring tribes.
CHOPWELL, a township in the p. of Ryton, in Durham, 11 m. from Gateshead. Area 3,150 acres. Pop. 320.

CHOQUEAPO, or RIO-LA-PAZ, a river of Upper Bolivia, which is supposed to rise on the W declivity of the Sorata, on the table-land of Cuzco, and to make its way eastwards to the Beni, which it joins on the l. bank.
CHORBROW, a village of Prussia, in Pomerania, 11 m. NW by N of Lauenburg, on an affluent of the Leba.
CHOREIBE, a town of Arabia, in Hadramaut, in that part of the Wadi Doan called Wadi Nebbi.
CHORESTI, a town of Turkey, in Lower Wallachia, 10 m. S by E of Foktchani, on the road to Brahilov.

CHORGES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Alpes, arrond. of Embrun.—The cant. comprises 8 com. Pop. in 1831, 5,076. — The com. is 10 m. W of Embrun. Pop. 2,009. Anthracite is dug, and building-stone quarried in the neighbourhood.

CHORILLOS, a village on the coast of Peru, in S lat. 12° 11'. It is built on a cliff or steep sandy beach, at the foot of a mountain, on a bay of the same name; and is resorted to as a bathing-place by the inhabitants of Lima. Not a tree is visible in the neighbourhood of the v., and the unshaded rays of the sun are reflected with twofold power from the hot sand, while the beach itself is rough and shingly. The place, however, is sheltered from the SW blasts by the elevated Morro-Solar; and during the raw, damp, and foggy months of July and August in Lima, C. enjoys a clear sky and a genial air.
CHORIN, a town of Prussia, in Brandenburg, in the gov. of Potsdam, and circle of Angermunde, 8 m. SE of Joachimsthal.

CHORIS PENINSULA, a low sandy promontory on the NW coast of America, in N lat. 67°, forming the W side of Eschscholtz bay, and running out towards Chamisso island, from which it is separated by a channel of 2 m. wide, with a depth varying from 1 to 9 fath.
CHORITITZA, a town of Greece, in the modern nomarchy of Laconia, on the r. bank of the Kastanias, an affluent of the Vasilico or Eurotas, 13 m. NW of Mistra.
CHORLA, a river of Sinde, which rises in the Kirtar mountains, and, after flowing N about 35 m., loses itself in the arid sands to the W of Sehwan.

CHORLEY, a township in the p. of Wilmslow, Cheshire, 6 m. NW of Macclesfield. Area 770 acres. Pop. 561.—Also a township in the p. of Wrenbury, Cheshire, 5 m. W of Nantwich. Area 1,280 acres. Pop. 183.—Also a p. and market-town in Lancashire, 32 m. SSE of Lancaster, 9 m. SSE of Preston. The town is intersected by the Great North road between Preston and Manchester; and is within a short distance of the C. station on the Preston and Bolton railway, by which it is 8 m. from Preston, 12 m. from Bolton, and 21 m. from Manchester. The majority of the inhabitants are cotton-spinners and weavers. Area of p. 3,110 acres. Pop. 13,139.

CHORLI, or CHORLU, a town of Turkey, in the prov. of Romelia, 16 m. NE by E of Rodosto. It consists of about 800 houses, chiefly inhabited by Turks. It stands upon an eminence near the l. bank of a river of the same name, which, descending from the Little Balkan, flows in a SW and WSW course of about 40 m. into the sea of Marmora, a few miles to the E of the port of Rodosto. The sea is visible from the town, bounded by the heights of the island of Marmora, and by the Tekir-dagh, under which Rodosto lies.

CHORLTON, a township in the p. of Malpas, in Cheshire, 7 m. NW of Whitchurch. Area 610 acres. Pop. 150. - Also a township in the p. of Backford, 4 m. N of Chester. Area 580 acres. Pop. 85.—Also a township in the p. of Wybunbury, 5 m. ESE of Nantwich. Area 700 acres. Pop. 141.— Also a chapelry in the p. of Eccleshall, in Stafford, 6 m. N of Eccleshall. Pop. 265.
CHORLTON-WITH-HARDY, a chapelry in the p. of Manchester, in Lancashire, 4 m. SW of Manchester. Area 1,400 acres. Pop. 632.
CHORLTON - HILL, a township in the p. of Eccleshall, Stafford. Pop. 265.

CHORLTON-ROW, a township in the p. of Manchester, in Lancashire, about 14 m. S of Manchester, and comprised within the boundaries of the parl. borough of Manchester. Area 700 acres. Pop. in 1801, 605; in 1841, 28,336.

CHORO', a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Ceara, rising in the Serra-de-Santa-Catharina, to the S of Quixeramobim, and falling into the ocean between the Rio-Formosa and the Ceara, after a NE course of about 50 m.
CHOROBROW, a village of Austria, in Galicia, in the circle of Zolkiev, near the S bank of the Bug.
CHOROL. See KHOROL.

CHOROLQUE, a nevado or snowy mountain of Bolivia, in S lat. 21° 30', NW of the city of Tupisa. Dr. Redhead estimates its alt. above sea-level at CHO 16,548 ft.; but Mr. Pentland thinks this estimate too low, because its pyramidal summit is visible from Bartolo, a v. NE of Potosi, distant in a straight line from this nevado at least 115 geog. m. The name is a corruption of the Quichua word churucolque, which signifies that the mountain contains silver and shells; and marine shells were discovered near its summit in 1826.

CHOROS, a small town of Chili, in the dep. and 30 m. N of Coquimbo, near the mouth of a small stream. Off C. point there are three islands known as the C. islands, of which the largest, about 2 m. in length, has its SW point in S lat. 29° 15' 45", W long. 71° 37′ 30″.
CHOROSTKOW, a large market-town of Austria, in Galicia, in the circle and 30 m. SSE of Tarnopol, on the l. bank of the Tayna, between Mikulince and Husiatyn. Pop. 2,400.
CHOROTAS, a tribe of S American Indians, inhabiting the S bank of the Meta, and the head-streams of the Vichada, in the dep. of Cazanare, in New Granada.

CHORRERA, a town of New Granada, in the dep. of Panama, on the Rio-Martin-Sanchez, a headstream of a river which takes its name from the town, and falls into the Pacific, about 10 m. W of Panama; and not far from the sources of the Trinidad, which, flowing towards the Caribbean sea, falls into the Chagres a little above its mouth. Pop. 4,000. See articles Chagres and Panama.
CHORU'K, or Chorokh, a river of Georgia, separating Guria, now belonging to Russia, from the pashalik of Trebisond, and flowing into the Black sea a little to the SW of Batum.

CHORU'M, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the pashalik of Sivas, 40 m. WSW of Amasia, on a small affluent of the Charshambah or Iris. It is rather a large town "of most bigoted Mussulmans" [W. J. Hamilton], situated in the midst of an extensive plain stretching N and S. Colonel Leake supposes it to represent the ancient Tavium.
CHORZELE, or CHORZELLEN, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Plock, obwod and 16 m. N of Przasnysz, on the Orsic, near the frontiers of Prussia. Pop. 1,700.

CHOTA-NAGPORE, a pergunnah of Bengal, 220 m. WNW of Calcutta. It is a hilly and jungly district, measuring about 95 m. by 80 m. The plains in some parts are extensive, and are generally well cultivated. The mountains are wholly covered with forests and underwood, in which the saul tree (Shorea robusta) seems to predominate. Rice, cotton, the sugar-cane, and various grains, are cultivated in the lower grounds; the hills produce lac, gum, and silk. Iron-ore is found; and gold is said to exist in the sands of some of the streams. It is governed by six subordinate rajas, under a chief raja whose revenues are said to amount to 400,000 rupees. A tribute of 26,000 rupees is paid to the Company.
CHOTAN, or Ch'ho'tan, a village and pergunnah of Marwar, in Central Hindostan It is a place of considerable antiquity, but quite decayed.
CHOTEILI', a village belonging to the Mekrani Patans, in Cutch-Gundava, on the route from Gulu to Talli. Wheat and rice are cultivated in the environs.
CHOTEN. See KOTHAN.
CHOTIEBORZ, a village of Austria, in Bohemia, in the circle and 18 m. SE of Czaslau, near the I. bank of the Dobrowa. Pop. 2,000.
CHOTIESCHAU, or Chottessow, a village of Austria, in Bohemia, in the circle and 11 m. SW of Pilten, at an alt. of 1,070 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,055.

CHOTISGHUR, or Ch'hattis-Garh, a large district of Hindostan, in Central Gundwana, subject to the raja of Nagpur. It is a hilly country, and thickly inhabited. Its cap., Ratnapur, is situated in N lat. 22° 21', E long. 82° 25'. Pop. 3,000. In 1824 it yielded a revenue of 385,840 rupees.
CHOTTA-GULLU, a village of Sinde, 45 m. N of Sehwan, 4 m. from the r. bank of the Indus, in N lat. 27°.
CHOTTA-SITA, a village of Sinde, 13 m. N of Chotta-Gullu, near the r. bank of the Indus.
CHOTUSITZ, a town of Austria, in Bohemia, in the circle and 2 m. N of Czaslau. Pop. 1,000. A battle was fought between the Prussians and Austrians at this place in 1742.

CHOTYAALI, a village of Afghanistan, in Seistan, in about 30° N lat., on the road from Deraford. Ghazi-Khan to Kandahar. CHOTZEN [Bohem. Chocen], a town of Austria, in Bohemia, in the circle and 20 m. E of Chrudim, on the Stille-Adler. Pop. 1,955. Prince Kinsky has a fine chateau here.
CHOUCHEH, a small town of Central Egypt, 15 m. SSW of Abu-Girgeh.
CHOUE', a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Loir-et-Cher, cant. of Mondoubleau, on the l. bank of the Braye. Pop. 1,122.
CHOUIA. See Schouia.
CHOUI-KIN, a town of China, in the prov. of Kiang-si, in N lat. 25° 49′, 60 m. E of Kan-tchou.
CHOUILLY, a commune of France, in the cant. of Epernay. Pop. 1,030.
CHOUAI-NGAU, a town and small port of China, in the prov. of Che-kiang, 20 m. S of Ouen-chou.
CHOUIOUK, one of the Fox islands, off the coast of Russian America, in N lat. 58° 35', to the N of Afognak.
CHOUI-PING-SE, a town of China, in the prov. of Sechwen, 46 m. W of Chun.
CHOUISKOI, a village of Russia, in the gov. of Olonetz, 12 m. N of Petrozavodsk.—Also a v. of Russia, in the gov. of Vologda, on the r. bank of the Sukhona, 40 m. ENE of Vologda.
